text:
Youth and the Imitatio Christi: Exploring Ways of Imitating Christ through the Coaching and Equipping of Children for Ministry In this original thesis the coaching and equipping of children for service is investigated as a paradigm for imitating Christ. Starting with the assumption that young people are an integral part of the body of Christ, it is argued that as members of the body of Christ children will receive gifts that are for the edification of the church. It is the church’s responsibility to accompany and equip children as they live out their calling as followers of Jesus.

In this research, the concept of *diakonia* or “service” is explored in relation to its historical roots, and the various and more developed forms that it may take in the present day. This research employs a four phase practical-theological methodology which describes and interpretively reflects on the research undertaken in the field, engages in an intra-disciplinary conversation with the field of social and developmental psychology in an attempt to determine what inhibits the spiritual development of a child – such as prejudice, parental or communal lack of nurture – explores the biblical texts in an attempt to build a theoretical foundation for an ideal *diakonia*, and finally draws on theoretical-practical conclusions as to how the church can assist a child in performing their *diakonia*.

The authors find that only children who serve as a service to the Lord Jesus are equipped to copy His world view. When parents serve as children's mentors and engage their children in “diaconal” service, not only are they teaching their children to serve but they are also playing a crucial role in the spiritual growth of their children.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
